questions.question: Which technique is used to improve Random Forest stability?

explanations.a: Bagging trains trees on different bootstrap samples and averages predictions, reducing variance and improving stability.
explanations.b: Bagging trains trees on different bootstrap samples and averages predictions, reducing variance and improving stability.
explanations.c: Bagging trains trees on different bootstrap samples and averages predictions, reducing variance and improving stability.
explanations.d: Bagging trains trees on different bootstrap samples and averages predictions, reducing variance and improving stability.

questions.question: Which is a key practical advantage of Random Forest over a single Decision Tree?

answers.a: Usually lower variance and better generalization
answers.d: Uses only one tree for prediction

explanations.a: By averaging many decorrelated trees, Random Forest generally reduces variance and improves generalization.
explanations.b: By averaging many decorrelated trees, Random Forest generally reduces variance and improves generalization.
explanations.c: By averaging many decorrelated trees, Random Forest generally reduces variance and improves generalization.
explanations.d: By averaging many decorrelated trees, Random Forest generally reduces variance and improves generalization.

questions.question: What happens to bias and variance in Random Forest compared to a single tree?

explanations.a: Random Forest slightly reduces bias and significantly reduces variance.
explanations.b: Random Forest slightly reduces bias and significantly reduces variance.
explanations.c: Random Forest significantly reduces variance by averaging many trees, while typically causing a slight increase in bias because individual trees are constrained by random feature subsets.
explanations.d: Random Forest slightly reduces bias and significantly reduces variance.

questions.question: Which Random Forest parameter controls feature randomness at each split?

explanations.a: max_features controls how many features are randomly considered at each split.
explanations.b: max_features controls how many features are randomly considered at each split.
explanations.c: max_features controls how many features are randomly considered at each split.
explanations.d: max_features controls how many features are randomly considered at each split.

questions.question: How does Random Forest handle noisy data compared to a single Decision Tree?

answers.c: It is more robust to noise

explanations.a: Averaging predictions reduces the influence of noisy samples.
explanations.b: Averaging predictions reduces the influence of noisy samples.
explanations.c: Averaging predictions reduces the influence of noisy samples.
explanations.d: Averaging predictions reduces the influence of noisy samples.

questions.question: Which output aggregation method is used in Random Forest regression?

explanations.a: Random Forest regression averages predictions from all trees.
explanations.b: Random Forest regression averages predictions from all trees.
explanations.c: Random Forest regression averages predictions from all trees.
explanations.d: Random Forest regression averages predictions from all trees.

questions.question: Why is Random Forest considered computationally expensive?

explanations.a: Multiple trees increase memory and computation requirements.
explanations.b: Multiple trees increase memory and computation requirements.
explanations.c: Multiple trees increase memory and computation requirements.
explanations.d: Multiple trees increase memory and computation requirements.

questions.question: Which scenario best suits Random Forest usage?

answers.a: Very small datasets with linear patterns

explanations.a: Random Forest handles high dimensionality and nonlinearity well.
explanations.b: Random Forest handles high dimensionality and nonlinearity well.
explanations.c: Random Forest handles high dimensionality and nonlinearity well.
explanations.d: Random Forest handles high dimensionality and nonlinearity well.

questions.question: What information can Random Forest provide besides predictions?

explanations.a: Random Forest estimates feature importance based on split contributions.
explanations.b: Random Forest estimates feature importance based on split contributions.
explanations.c: Random Forest estimates feature importance based on split contributions.
explanations.d: Random Forest estimates feature importance based on split contributions.

questions.question: Why is Random Forest preferred over boosting in some cases?

answers.a: It focuses on hard samples only
answers.b: It is less sensitive to noise

explanations.a: Boosting may overfit noisy data, while Random Forest is more stable.
explanations.b: Boosting may overfit noisy data, while Random Forest is more stable.
explanations.c: Boosting may overfit noisy data, while Random Forest is more stable.
explanations.d: Boosting may overfit noisy data, while Random Forest is more stable.

questions.question: What does the Out-of-Bag (OOB) score in Random Forest estimate?

answers.c: Generalization performance using samples not seen by each tree
answers.d: Number of features selected at each split

explanations.a: OOB uses predictions from trees where a sample was not included during training, giving a built-in validation estimate.
explanations.b: OOB uses predictions from trees where a sample was not included during training, giving a built-in validation estimate.
explanations.c: OOB uses predictions from trees where a sample was not included during training, giving a built-in validation estimate.
explanations.d: OOB uses predictions from trees where a sample was not included during training, giving a built-in validation estimate.

questions.question: In Random Forest, increasing n_estimators from 50 to 300 usually has what effect?

answers.a: Often improves stability/accuracy but with diminishing returns and more computation
answers.c: Reduces model size and training time

explanations.a: More trees can improve ensemble stability and performance, but gains typically taper off while cost increases.
explanations.b: More trees can improve ensemble stability and performance, but gains typically taper off while cost increases.
explanations.c: More trees can improve ensemble stability and performance, but gains typically taper off while cost increases.
explanations.d: More trees can improve ensemble stability and performance, but gains typically taper off while cost increases.

questions.question: How does reducing max_features typically influence a Random Forest?

answers.a: It can increase tree diversity and reduce correlation among trees
answers.b: It removes the need for bootstrap sampling
answers.c: It guarantees higher accuracy for every dataset
answers.d: It converts Random Forest into boosting

explanations.a: Considering fewer features per split increases randomness, often making trees less correlated.
explanations.b: Considering fewer features per split increases randomness, often making trees less correlated.
explanations.c: Considering fewer features per split increases randomness, often making trees less correlated.
explanations.d: Considering fewer features per split increases randomness, often making trees less correlated.

questions.question: What does a high feature importance value indicate in a trained Random Forest?

answers.a: That feature contributes strongly to impurity reduction across trees
answers.b: That feature should always be removed
answers.c: That feature was never used in splits

explanations.a: Feature importance reflects cumulative contribution to splitting quality (e.g., impurity reduction).
explanations.b: Feature importance reflects cumulative contribution to splitting quality (e.g., impurity reduction).
explanations.c: Feature importance reflects cumulative contribution to splitting quality (e.g., impurity reduction).
explanations.d: Feature importance reflects cumulative contribution to splitting quality (e.g., impurity reduction).
